evidence: All 9 toolkit tools (publish, reply, react, tip, scan, verify, attest, discoverSources, pay) follow the same (session, opts) => Promise<ToolResult<T>> signature pattern
Every tool uses withToolWrapper for timing, session.touch(), observer notification, and error wrapping — zero boilerplate duplication
Zod validation via validateInput() at every tool entry point ensures consistent input checking
Each tool has its own typed XxxOptions interface (ReactOptions, TipOptions, etc.) — no overloaded or ambiguous parameter bags
Guard APIs follow consistent checkAndRecordXxx(store, walletAddress, ..., record: boolean) => Promise<DemosError | null> pattern
connect/disconnect intentionally use throw/void semantics (documented deviation for lifecycle vs tool operations)
session.logger referenced in scan.ts lines 68 and 75 but logger property is never declared on DemosSession — optional chaining makes calls silently no-op
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: Phantom session.logger property in scan.ts. ConnectOptions has 13 fields (large config surface). SdkBridge interface has 13+ methods which approaches the boundary of a coherent interface.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit API surface is remarkably coherent. A developer encountering any one tool can immediately predict the shape of all others — consistent (session, opts) => ToolResult<T> pattern, uniform Zod validation, shared withToolWrapper boilerplate, and typed per-tool options interfaces. The connect/disconnect deviation from ToolResult to throw/void is intentional, documented, and semantically justified. The only friction point is a phantom logger property on DemosSession that silently no-ops.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The API surface approaches exemplary coherence. All tools share a predictable, type-safe contract, and the guard layer follows its own consistent pattern. The withToolWrapper abstraction eliminates cross-cutting boilerplate without adding indirection. The phantom session.logger and the growing SdkBridge interface are minor friction — they represent isolated rough edges rather than systemic inconsistency. This places the score solidly in the 88-90 range.

issues.summary: scan.ts references session.logger which is never declared on DemosSession
evidence: scan.ts lines 68 and 75 call session.logger?.warn?.(...) but DemosSession has no logger property
Optional chaining prevents runtime errors but the calls are dead code — no warnings are ever emitted
issues.suggestion: Either add an optional logger property to DemosSession (e.g., logger?: { warn?: (...args: unknown[]) => void }) and wire it through ConnectOptions, or replace the calls with console.warn to match the pattern used in react.ts line 44.

add.description: All 9 toolkit tools follow the same function signature pattern with typed options and typed result envelopes, making API shape fully predictable from a single example.
Timing, session.touch(), observer notification, and error wrapping are centralized in a single wrapper. Every tool delegates to it, ensuring consistent behavior without code duplication.

add.header: Consistent Zod validation at every entry point
add.description: Every tool calls validateInput(Schema, opts) as its first operation, providing uniform input validation and consistent INVALID_INPUT error shapes.
All guards (write-rate, dedup, tip-spend) use a consistent checkAndRecordXxx(store, walletAddress, ..., record: boolean) => Promise<DemosError | null> contract.
connect() returns DemosSession directly (throws on failure) and disconnect() returns void — documented as lifecycle operations distinct from ToolResult-returning tools. Semantically appropriate.
